text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY
FOLLOW-UP OFFICE VISIT — METASTATIC HORMONE-SENSITIVE PROSTATE CANCER

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology / Genitourinary Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male (presenting age 68 at diagnosis) of Polish-American descent, a retired ironworker, who presents for scheduled 3-month follow-up after initiating androgen deprivation therapy with Lupron acetate and abi acetate/prednisone for newly diagnosed metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC). He reports feeling substantially better overall and is here primarily for his PSA check, labs, and medication review.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed approximately 3 months ago with Stage IVB prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, 5+4, Grade Group 5) following presentation with lower urinary tract symptoms and a markedly elevated PSA of 187.4 ng/mL. Staging workup at that time demonstrated osseous metastases involving L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala on whole-body Tc-99m bone scintigraphy, as well as enlarged right external iliac lymph nodes (largest 1.8 cm on CT imaging). No visceral metastases were identified at that time. Transrectal ultrasound-guided needle core biopsy confirmed high-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma with 10 of 12 cores involved, perineural invasion present, and lymphovascular invasion present. Clinical staging was T3bN1M1b.

He was initiated on leup acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months as the backbone of androgen deprivation, combined with Zytiga acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally daily and prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily. This combination was chosen based on the OS benefit demonstrated in the LATITUDE trial for high-risk mHSPC and further supported by data from the STAMPEDE trial. At LHRH agonist initiation, bicalutamide 50 mg daily was administered for 4 weeks as flare prophylaxis to prevent early testosterone surge.

At today's visit, the patient is notably improved compared to his presentation at diagnosis. His most troubling symptom at that time was diffuse lower back pain and right pelvic girdle pain, which he rated 7/10 in severity, requiring opioid-containing analgesics and significantly impacting his sleep and quality of life. He reports that pain has declined markedly — he now rates it 2/10 and is managing without opioids. He is sleeping better, his appetite has returned, and he has been able to resume daily walks of 20-30 minutes. He reports taking his abi on an empty stomach as instructed and has not missed doses. He received his second leup injection approximately 4 weeks ago without any injection-site complications. He denies new focal bone pain, neurologic symptoms (no weakness, numbness, or bowel/bladder changes), fevers, or weight loss.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Improved energy overall compared to presentation, though mild persistent fatigue present, which he attributes to the hormonal therapy. Denies significant weight loss; reports slight weight gain of approximately 2 lbs over 3 months. No night sweats.
Musculoskeletal: Back pain dramatically improved as described (2/10 from 7/10 at diagnosis). Right pelvic/iliac crest discomfort essentially resolved. No new joint pain or swelling. Reports mild morning stiffness, which he finds manageable.
Genitourinary: Notes modest improvement in urinary flow since starting treatment, likely reflecting prostate volume reduction in response to ADT. Denies urinary incontinence, hesitancy, retention, dysuria, or hematuria. Libido absent — patient was extensively counseled on this at treatment initiation and accepts it as expected.
Neurological: Denies new weakness, numbness, or paresthesias in extremities. Denies headaches, visual changes, or dizziness. No symptoms to suggest spinal cord compromise.
Cardiovascular: Occasional mild bilateral ankle swelling present but not worsening. Denies exertional chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea at rest. Hypertension has been stable per home blood pressure log.
Endocrine/metabolic: Hot flushes moderate in frequency, approximately 5–8 per day, which the patient finds bothersome but not intolerable. On venlafaxine for hot flash management with partial benefit. Denies symptoms of adrenal insufficiency such as orthostatic dizziness or hypotension.
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea in the first 2–3 weeks after starting abi, now fully resolved. No vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, or abdominal pain at present.
Psychosocial/Psychiatric: Mood improved significantly since diagnosis. Active engagement with family and social activities. Denies depression or anxiety requiring formal psychiatric intervention. Patient remains positive about his treatment course.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Stage IVB, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5 — approximately 3 months ago
2. Hypertension — longstanding, well controlled on lisinopril
3. Hyperlipidemia — on statin therapy, well controlled
4. Mild bilateral osteopenia — identified on DEXA scan obtained at time of ADT initiation (recommended given anticipated long-term ADT)
5. No prior prostate procedures (no prior TURP, prostatectomy, or radiation)
6. No prior malignancies

FAMILY HISTORY:
Father: prostate cancer, died at age 78
Paternal aunt: ovarian cancer (age of onset unknown)
No known history of breast cancer, pancreatic cancer, or Lynch syndrome-associated malignancies in the family, though detailed multi-generational history not yet formally obtained
Comment: Given the patient's high-grade/high-volume presentation and family history of prostate and ovarian cancer (potentially suggestive of an hereditary BRCA2 or other HRR gene predisposition syndrome), germline genetic counseling referral has been placed.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Retired ironworker with approximately 35 years in structural steel and construction. Lifetime non-smoker. Occasional alcohol use (1–2 drinks per week, no binge drinking). No illicit substance use. Married; lives at home with wife. Two adult children who are supportive and involved. Patient is alert, independent in all ADLs, and engaged in his own care. Insurance: Medicare. Enrolled in manufacturer co-pay assistance program (JCAP) for abi to offset out-of-pocket costs.

ALLERGIES:
Penicillin — rash in childhood; exact nature uncertain; has tolerated cephalosporins without incident in adulthood
No known allergies to chemotherapy or oncology-specific agents

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. leup acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months
2. Zytiga acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally once daily on empty stomach
3. Prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily (with Zytiga)
4. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily
5. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally at bedtime
6. Calcium carbonate 1200 mg orally daily with vitamin D 1000 IU (bone health on ADT)
7. Venlafaxine 37.5 mg orally once daily (hot flash management on ADT — partial response)
8. Acetaminophen 500 mg orally PRN (pain — using rarely now, approximately 1–2 times per week)
Recently discontinued: Bicalutamide 50 mg daily (4-week flare prophylaxis course completed at ADT initiation)
Recently discontinued: Acetaminophen-codeine (no longer required given improvement in pain)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 126/78 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 98.4°F orally, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight: 174 lbs (BMI 26.6; baseline BMI at diagnosis 26.4). Height: 5'9".

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished male in no acute distress. Mood pleasant and conversational. No pallor, jaundice, or diaphoresis.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae without pallor. Mucous membranes moist. No cervical, supraclavicular, or occipital lymphadenopathy palpated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Mild 1+ bilateral ankle pitting edema, present at prior visits and unchanged. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally in radial, femoral, and dorsalis pedis distributions.

Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Respiratory effort unlabored.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds normoactive in all four quadrants.

Genitourinary: Deferred at this visit (prostate examination not routinely repeated at monitoring follow-up in the metastatic setting).

Musculoskeletal: No midline spinal tenderness to direct palpation over the lumbar vertebrae. No paraspinal muscle spasm. Mild residual tenderness to deep palpation over the right iliac crest (known metastatic site), notably improved compared to prior. Gait normal. No antalgic posture. Full range of motion at hips and knees.

Neurological: Alert and fully oriented to person, place, and time. Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation intact to light touch in all four extremities. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No evidence of myelopathic or cauda equina signs. Coordination intact.

Skin: No rash, petechiae, ecchymosis, or suspicious lesions. Mild facial flushing noted at rest, consistent with vasomotor hot flashes from ADT-related hypogonadism. No gynecomastia detected on palpation at this 3-month mark (though patients may develop gynecomastia later in the course of ADT; counsel patient accordingly).

Lymph Nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or epitrochlear lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today):

Tumor Markers:
  PSA: 4.2 ng/mL
    (Prior: 187.4 ng/mL at diagnosis — 97.8% decline; robust PSA response)
  Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (confirmed castrate; therapeutic goal <50 ng/dL)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  Sodium: 139 mEq/L
  Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L (no hypokalemia — important monitoring parameter on Zytiga given mineralocorticoid excess mechanism)
  CO2: 25 mEq/L
  Chloride: 102 mEq/L
  BUN: 18 mg/dL
  Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 72 mL/min/1.73m2 — stable renal function)
  Glucose: 96 mg/dL (no hyperglycemia despite concurrent prednisone use; monitoring will continue)
  AST: 22 U/L (normal)
  ALT: 28 U/L (normal)
  Alkaline phosphatase: 98 U/L (markedly decreased from 312 U/L at diagnosis — reflects meaningful improvement in osseous disease activity/bone remodeling)
  Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
  Albumin: 3.9 g/dL

Complete Blood Count with Differential:
  WBC: 6.2 K/uL (normal differential, no lymphopenia)
  Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (mild decrease from presumed pre-treatment baseline; normochromic, normocytic; ADT-associated mild anemia expected)
  MCV: 89 fL
  Platelets: 218 K/uL

Lipid Panel:
  LDL: 88 mg/dL (well controlled on atorvastatin)
  HDL: 42 mg/dL
  Triglycerides: 148 mg/dL
  Total cholesterol: 178 mg/dL
  Note: ADT is associated with adverse lipid changes over time; statin continuation appropriate.

Additional:
  Blood pressure confirmed 126/78 at visit (well controlled; Zytiga can occasionally exacerbate hypertension via mineralocorticoid pathway; current lisinopril regimen adequate)

No imaging performed at today's visit. Next formal restaging with bone scan and CT is planned at approximately 12 months on therapy, per standard monitoring protocol for mHSPC on systemic therapy, or sooner if symptoms suggest progression.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. METASTATIC HORMONE-SENSITIVE PROSTATE CANCER (mHSPC), STAGE IVB, GLEASON 9 (5+4) — MAJOR BIOCHEMICAL RESPONSE TO ADT + abi AT 3 MONTHS

Mr. Kowalski has had an outstanding early response to intensified ADT. His PSA has fallen from 187.4 ng/mL at diagnosis to 4.2 ng/mL today, a 97.8% reduction achieved in just 3 months. This depth of PSA response is clinically meaningful — patients achieving PSA <4 ng/mL or greater than 90% decline at 3–6 months on combined ADT therapy in the mHSPC setting have demonstrated favorable outcomes in multiple landmark datasets, including analyses from LATITUDE and STAMPEDE. Testosterone is confirmed in the deeply castrate range at <10 ng/dL, confirming the adequacy of LHRH agonist therapy and ruling out castration failure as a confounding factor. Clinically, bone pain has improved from 7/10 to 2/10, performance status is maintained at ECOG 1, and alkaline phosphatase has normalized, all convergent indicators of meaningful disease response.

Plan:
  - Continue leup acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months; confirm and schedule next injection date
  - Continue abi acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally once daily on empty stomach; reinforce dietary instructions (fasting critical for bioavailability — taking with food dramatically reduces absorption and efficacy)
  - Continue prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily concurrently with abi; patient reminded not to abruptly discontinue
  - PSA nadir expected to continue declining over coming months; nadir typically reached between 6 and 12 months of therapy
  - Repeat PSA, testosterone, and CMP (including potassium, liver enzymes) in 6 weeks
  - Formal restaging imaging (Tc-99m bone scan + CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) scheduled at 12-month mark; will proceed sooner if there is clinical suspicion of progression (new or worsening bone pain, rising PSA on two consecutive measurements, or new symptoms)

2. abi/PREDNISONE TOLERABILITY AND SAFETY MONITORING

The patient is tolerating the current regimen well with no significant adverse effects at this point. Key safety parameters reviewed:
  - Hypokalemia: None (K+ 4.1 mEq/L). Zytiga inhibits CYP17A1, leading to accumulation of upstream mineralocorticoid precursors that can cause hypokalemia and fluid retention; prednisone co-administration partially mitigates this. Monthly potassium monitoring to continue.
  - Hepatotoxicity: None (AST 22, ALT 28, all within normal limits). Liver enzyme monitoring per schedule (monthly for first 3 months, then every 3 months thereafter if stable).
  - Fluid retention/hypertension: Mild bilateral ankle edema unchanged from baseline; blood pressure well controlled on lisinopril. No medication changes needed at this time.
  - Hyperglycemia: None on prednisone (glucose 96 fasting). Continued monitoring with CMP.
  - Hot flushes: Moderate frequency (5–8/day), partially managed with venlafaxine 37.5 mg. Consider titrating venlafaxine to 75 mg daily at next visit if hot flashes remain bothersome; alternatively, can discuss acupuncture or medroxyprogesterone as adjuncts.

3. BONE HEALTH

Patient has pre-existing bilateral mild osteopenia identified on DEXA scan obtained at time of ADT initiation. Prolonged ADT accelerates bone mineral density loss (approximately 2–3% per year), and he additionally carries bone metastatic disease at multiple sites (L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala). Currently on calcium carbonate 1200 mg/day and vitamin D 1000 IU/day as baseline supplementation. The role of bone-modifying agents (zoledronic acid or denosumab) in the hormone-sensitive metastatic setting is evolving; in the CRPC setting, denosumab 120 mg every 4 weeks is FDA-approved for skeletal event prevention. Will revisit this at next visit with updated guideline review. Ensure vitamin D sufficiency at next labs. Advise continued weight-bearing activity as tolerated.

4. GERMLINE GENETIC COUNSELING — REFERRAL PLACED

Given the patient's diagnosis with high-grade, metastatic prostate cancer at age 68 and notable family history (father: prostate cancer; paternal aunt: ovarian cancer), referral to genetic counseling has been formally placed. Per current NCCN, AUA, and ESMO guidelines, germline testing for BRCA1/2, ATM, CDK12, CHEK2, and other HRR-pathway genes is recommended for patients with metastatic, high-grade, or early-onset prostate cancer. Identification of a germline BRCA2 or other HRR pathogenic variant would have implications both for the patient's own future treatment options (PARP inhibitor eligibility, platinum sensitivity) and for at-risk family members who should undergo cascade genetic testing. Patient is aware of this referral and is agreeable.

5. GOALS OF CARE AND LONGITUDINAL PLANNING

At this stage, the disease is demonstrating a strong response to first-line intensified ADT and the prognosis in mHSPC remains guarded (median OS approximately 3–4 years with high-risk features and Zytiga-based therapy in LATITUDE, though individual variation is significant). Patient and wife appear well informed and engaged. No urgent goals-of-care discussions required at this visit given excellent response and maintained functional status. Will continue longitudinal counseling. At such time as the disease becomes castration-resistant — defined biochemically as three consecutive PSA rises from nadir on castrate testosterone, or radiographic progression — treatment re-stratification will be necessary. In the event of castration resistance, options including second-line AR pathway inhibition (enza or darolutamide), PARP inhibitors (dependent on HRR gene mutation status), taxane chemotherapy, or other approved agents would be considered. The current excellent response is the first priority.

6. SUPPORTIVE CARE AND QUALITY OF LIFE

Patient is independent in ADLs, living at home, and physically active. He has been counseled on the importance of aerobic exercise and resistance training during ADT to mitigate the well-documented metabolic and musculoskeletal sequelae of testosterone suppression (sarcopenia, osteopenia, insulin resistance, fatigue, and cardiovascular risk). Patient expresses interest in an exercise oncology program; referral to physical therapy and/or supervised exercise program placed. Diet counseling for heart-healthy diet discussed in the context of ADT-associated metabolic syndrome risk. No formal palliative care consultation needed at this visit given good symptom control and ECOG PS 1.

RETURN VISIT: 6 weeks for PSA/testosterone recheck and metabolic panel (potassium, liver enzymes); 3-month comprehensive follow-up thereafter. leup injection to be scheduled at appropriate interval.

Total visit time: approximately 40 minutes, including face-to-face assessment, lab review, and counseling.

Electronically signed and attested by: [Attending Physician, Medical Oncology / Genitourinary Oncology]
